
### really contrived?  {.smaller}


```
char *array1; char *array2;
if (x < array1_size)
    y = array2[array1[x] * 4096];

```
 

* times 4096 shifts so we can get lower bits of target value 

   * so all bits effect what cache block is used

 <hr />
 
<div class="fragment fade-in" data-fragment-index=2 >
 
```
int *array1; int *array2;
if (x < array1_size)
    y = array2[array1[x]];

```
 

* will still get <i>upper</i> bits of array1[x] (can tell from cache set) 

   * still likely to be sensitive data

 
</div>


### bounds check in kernel  {.smaller}

<!-- \lstset{
    language=C,
    style=small,
    moredelim={**[is][\btHL<2>]{@2}{2@}},
    moredelim={**[is][\btHL<3>]{@3}{3@}},
    moredelim={**[is][\btHL<4>]{@4}{4@}},
    moredelim={**[is][\btHL<5>]{@5}{5@}},
} -->
 
<pre><code>void SomeSystemCallHandler(int index) {
    if (<span data-fragment-index="2" class="fragment custom myem-only">index &gt; some_table_size</span>) 
        return ERROR;
    int kind = <span data-fragment-index="3" class="fragment custom myem-only">table[index]</span>;
    switch (<span data-fragment-index="4" class="fragment custom myem-only">other_table[kind].foo</span>) {
        ...
    }
}
</code></pre>
 <hr />
 
<pre><code>if (<span data-fragment-index="2" class="fragment custom myem-only">x &lt; array1_size</span>) {
    y = <span data-fragment-index="4" class="fragment custom myem-only">array2[<span data-fragment-index="3" class="fragment custom myem-only">array1[x]</span>]</span>;
}
</code></pre>


### generalizing exploit 


* limited in what address we can learn about based on how big entries in tables are 

   * but can combine multiple Spectre-type exploits
   * only need one secret value leaked

* need to adjust calculations to actual addresses / array element sizes / etc.
